title: A Meta-analysis of Nontraditional Uses of Deep Inferior Epigastric Artery Perforator Flaps.
abstract: Background The deep inferior epigastric artery perforator (DIEP) flap is well established for breast reconstruction, yet its use in other anatomical regions remains insufficiently characterized. Existing literature consists largely of narrative reviews and isolated case reports. Therefore, we conducted a systematic review to evaluate the effectiveness of the DIEP flap in nonbreast reconstructive surgery. Methods A systematic search was performed following PRISMA guidelines. Of 328 initial titles, 117 articles underwent full-text review, and 89 studies met inclusion criteria. Results A total of 467 patients underwent nonbreast reconstruction using the DIEP flap. The mean age was 46.24 (range, 19-78) years. Reconstructions were performed in the head and neck (n = 122), lower limb (n = 115), upper limb (n = 95), vaginal region (n = 72), hip and thigh (n = 41), and trunk (n = 22). Nineteen total flap losses were reported, yielding an overall pooled survival rate of 96%. Region-specific survival rates were as follows: head and neck, 97%; lower limb, 93%; upper limb, 95%; vaginal, 95%; hip and thigh, 98%; and trunk, 95%. Donor-site morbidity was low, with only 13 cases (2.78%) reporting complications; the remaining 454 (97.22%) patients achieved primary donor-site closure without adverse events. Conclusions This systematic review demonstrates that the DIEP flap is a reliable option for reconstruction beyond the breast, with consistently high survival rates across multiple anatomical regions and minimal donor-site morbidity. These findings support the DIEP flap as a versatile and effective reconstructive tool, particularly in complex cases requiring durable soft tissue coverage and favorable functional outcomes.

journal: Plastic and reconstructive surgery. Global open
citation: Plastic and reconstructive surgery. Global open. 2026;14(6):e7837

full_text_sections.INTRODUCTION: INTRODUCTION Reconstruction of the breast with vascularized tissue transfer following mastectomy has become the standard surgical approach for many patients. Among the most commonly utilized free flaps for postmastectomy breast reconstruction is the deep inferior epigastric artery perforator (DIEP) flap. First described by Koshima and Soeda in 1989, 1 the DIEP flap harvests skin and adipose tissue from the lower abdomen while preserving the rectus abdominis muscle. It has become a preferred option for breast reconstruction due to superior aesthetic results, minimal donor site morbidity, and improved patient quality of life. 2 , 3 Additionally, by sparing the abdominal muscle and fascia, the DIEP flap is associated with significantly reduced postoperative pain. 4 Although its benefits in breast reconstruction are well established, the DIEP flap remains relatively underutilized and less well characterized for nonbreast reconstructions. Flap choice in complex reconstruction often depends on tissue composition, pedicle length, and donor site morbidity, making the DIEP flap’s attributes—such as a long, reliable pedicle, robust vascular supply, use of autologous tissue, and low risk of donor site hernia—highly advantageous for broader applications. 5 , 6 Guinier et al 7 offered a narrative overview describing its use in various nonbreast indications, but no quantitative synthesis has been conducted to date.
full_text_sections.PURPOSE: PURPOSE The purpose of this study was to perform a systematic review and meta-analysis of the available literature to evaluate the effectiveness, safety, and clinical outcomes of the DIEP flap in nonbreast reconstructions. By providing pooled survival rates, complication rates, and region-specific outcomes, this study aimed to clarify the flap’s role and expand evidence-based indications for its use beyond breast reconstruction.
full_text_sections.METHODS: METHODS A systematic literature search was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses 2020 statement. The PubMed, Embase, and Ovid MEDLINE databases were queried using predefined keywords related to nonbreast reconstruction applications of the DIEP flap. Inclusion criteria were defined as primary studies reporting on patients aged 18 years or older who underwent DIEP flap reconstruction for traumatic injury or defect in anatomical regions other than the breast. Exclusion criteria included duplicate publications, case reports, conference abstracts, simulation studies, review articles, animal studies, patients undergoing DIEP flap exclusively for breast reconstruction, and studies reporting exclusively on other autologous flap techniques. ( See appendix, Supplemental Digital Content 1 , which displays Ovid database search strategy, https://links.lww.com/PRSGO/E972 .) Bibliographies of included studies were also manually screened to identify additional eligible publications. Study selection adhered to a predefined protocol: titles and abstracts were initially screened, followed by full-text eligibility assessment. Disagreements between reviewers were resolved by consensus or consultation with a senior reviewer. Data extraction was performed independently by two reviewers using a standardized Microsoft Excel spreadsheet. Extracted variables included publication year, study design, number of patients, mean patient age, anatomical area of reconstruction, donor site morbidity, flap survival rates, and postoperative complications such as deep vein thrombosis or infection. Studies were not excluded based on risk-of-bias scores to maximize the sample size given the limited availability of data in this specific domain; however, statistical heterogeneity was assessed to ensure the robustness of the pooled results.
full_text_sections.STATISTICAL ANALYSIS: STATISTICAL ANALYSIS All statistical analyses were performed using R statistical software. A single-arm meta-analysis of proportions was conducted to calculate pooled event rates for flap survival and donor site morbidity. To weigh individual studies appropriately, the inverse variance method was utilized. Additionally, a random-effects model was used for all pooled analyses to account for potential variation between studies. Heterogeneity was assessed using the I 2 statistic and Cochran Q test.
full_text_sections.RESULTS: RESULTS Study Identification The initial database search identified 328 records. After title and abstract screening, 117 articles were assessed in full, and 89 studies met inclusion criteria for the systematic review and meta-analysis. Of these, 80 studies (89.9%) were found via electronic databases, whereas 9 (10.1%) were identified through bibliographic searches. Study designs included 45 case series, 32 case reports, and 12 retrospective reviews. The study selection process is illustrated in Figure 1 . Due to reference limits, a complete bibliography of the studies included in this meta-analysis is illustrated in each subgroup’s respective forest plot and is available from the corresponding author upon request. Fig. 1. Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses flow diagram summarizing the study identification and selection process (n = 89 included studies). Search Strategy Detailed database strategies are summarized in Supplemental Digital Content 1 ( https://links.lww.com/PRSGO/E972 ). The combined search of Ovid MEDLINE and Embase Classic+Embase using terms related to DIEP flaps outside the breast region yielded a total of 355 articles. Descriptive Analysis A total of 467 patients underwent DIEP flap reconstruction in nonbreast anatomical sites. The mean patient age was 46.2 years (range, 19–78 y). Reconstructions included: head and neck (n = 122), lower limb (n = 115), upper limb (n = 95), vaginal (n = 72), hip and thigh (n = 41), and trunk (n = 22). Details are presented in Table 1 . Table 1. Descriptive Analysis of the 467 DIEP Flaps Included in the Meta-analysis Subgroup No. Patients (n) No. Studies Mean Age (y) Flap Survival Rate (%) (95% CI) Flap Failures, n (%) Minor Donor Site Morbidity, n (%) Major Donor Site Morbidity, n (%) Head and neck 122 25 49.6 (22–64) 97 (92–100) 4 (3.3) 1 (0.8) 2 (1.6) Lower limb 115 32 41.6 (19–78) 93 (85–100) 8 (7.0) 2 (1.7) 0 (0) Upper limb 95 16 36.5 (23–54) 95 (90–100) 5 (5.3) 2 (2.2) 1 (1.1) Vagina 72 10 51.5 (23–72) 95 (89–100) 2 (2.8) 3 (4.2) 2 (2.8) Hip and thigh 41 16 53.6 (30–77) 98 (90–100) 0 (0) 0 (0) 1 (2.4) Trunk 22 6 54.2 (45–70) 95 (90–100) 0 (0) 0 (0) 0 (0) Total/overall 467 89 46.2 (19–78) 96 (pooled) 19 (4.1) 8 (1.7) 6 (1.3) The etiology of defects was heterogeneous across anatomical sites. Although trauma was the predominant indication for upper and lower limb reconstruction, oncological resection was more common in head, neck, and vaginal defects, with additional cases resulting from chronic infection (eg, osteomyelitis, Fournier gangrene) and congenital deformities. Flap Survival Rates The pooled overall flap survival rate was 96%, with 19 flap failures (4.1%). Subgroup survival rates were as follows: ● Head and neck: 97% (95% CI, 92%–100%; Fig. 2 ). ● Lower limb: 93% (95% CI, 85%–100%; Fig. 3 ). ● Upper limb: 95% (95% CI, 90%–100%; Fig. 4 ). ● Vagina: 95% (95% CI, 89%–100%; Fig. 5 ). ● Hip and thigh: 98% (95% CI, 90%–100%; Fig. 6 ). ● Trunk: 95% (95% CI, 90%–100%; Fig. 7 ). Fig. 2. Forest plot depicting meta-analysis of DIEP flap in head and neck reconstructions. Fig. 3. Forest plot depicting meta-analysis of DIEP flap in lower limb reconstructions. Fig. 4. Forest plot depicting meta-analysis of DIEP flap in upper limb reconstructions. Fig. 5. Forest plot depicting meta-analysis of DIEP flap in vaginal reconstructions. Fig. 6. Forest plot depicting meta-analysis of DIEP flap in hip and thigh reconstructions. Fig. 7. Forest plot depicting meta-analysis of DIEP flap in trunk reconstructions. Comparisons across subgroups showed no statistically significant difference ( P = 0.94). No significant heterogeneity was observed ( I 2 = 0, P = 1.00). Funnel plot demonstrated minimal asymmetry, suggesting low publication bias (Fig. 8 ). Fig. 8. Funnel plot assessing publication bias for flap survival outcomes. Donor Site Morbidity Donor site morbidity occurred in 13 patients (2.78%). Of these, minor complications included wound dehiscence or infection (n = 8, 1.7%); major complications included large dehiscence requiring reoperation or hematoma (n = 6, 1.3%). The remaining 454 patients (97.22%) had primary closure without complication. A secondary outcome meta-analysis revealed no significant difference in donor site morbidity rates between anatomical subgroups ( P = 0.86), with no significant heterogeneity ( I 2 = 0, P = 1.00; χ 2 = 1.9). Results are shown in Figure 9 , and funnel plot analysis (Fig. 10 ) indicated low publication bias. Fig. 9. Forest plot summarizing overall donor site morbidity. Fig. 10. Funnel plot assessing publication bias for donor site morbidity. Head and Neck This subgroup included 122 patients across 25 studies. The pooled flap survival rate was 97% (95% CI: 92%–100%) using a random-effects model (Fig. 2 ). The mean patient age was 49.6 years (range: 22–64 y). Lower Limb There were 115 patients from 32 studies included in the lower limb subgroup. The pooled flap survival rate was 93% (95% CI: 85%–100%) (Fig. 3 ). Mean patient age was 41.6 years (range: 19–78 y). Upper Limb This group included 95 patients from 16 studies. The flap survival rate was 95% (95% CI: 90%–100%) (Fig. 4 ). Mean patient age was 36.5 years (range: 23–54 y). Vagina The vaginal subgroup consisted of 72 patients across 10 studies. The pooled flap survival rate was 95% (95% CI: 89%–100%) (Fig. 5 ). Mean age was 51.5 years (range: 23–72 y). Hip and Thigh In this subgroup, 41 patients from 16 studies were included. The flap survival rate was 98% (95% CI: 90%–100%) (Fig. 6 ). Mean patient age was 53.6 years (range: 30–77 y). Trunk The trunk subgroup included 22 patients from 6 studies. The flap survival rate was 95% (95% CI: 90%–100%) (Fig. 7 ). Mean age was 54.2 years (range: 45–70 y). Postoperative Complications An overall complication rate of 14.56% (n = 68) was observed. Most common were venous thrombosis or congestion (16.18%) and wound dehiscence (16.18%), followed by infection (10.3%). Additional subgroup-specific complications included 7 cases of vaginal fistula and 4 cases of vaginal stenosis.
full_text_sections.DISCUSSION: DISCUSSION Flap choice requires balancing defect coverage, vascular reliability, and morbidity. Since its description by Koshima and Soeda, 1 the DIEP flap has become standard for breast reconstruction due to its robust vascularity and muscle-sparing nature. However, its broader application beyond the breast remains less characterized. Our meta-analysis included 467 patients across six nonbreast regions and showed an overall DIEP flap survival rate of 96%, comparable to reported breast reconstruction survival rates (98.2%). 8 Subgroup survival rates were also comparable to commonly used regional free flaps: for example, head and neck (range, 93.1%–98%) 9 , 10 ; lower limb (95%) 11 ; upper limb (94.7%) 12 ; vaginal reconstruction (96.8%) 13 ; hip/thigh (86%–100%) 14 , 15 ; and trunk (95.8%–96.7%). 16 , 17 No significant difference in flap survival among subgroups ( P = 0.94) and lack of heterogeneity (I 2 = 0, P = 1.00) suggest that the DIEP flap is robust across anatomical sites. Advantages ● Large tissue volume and design flexibility: allows coverage of complex, large, or circumferential defects. 18 ● Long pedicle length: facilitates anastomosis to distant recipient vessels, critical in irradiated fields or head and neck defects. 19 ● Dual venous drainage: enhances outflow, reducing risk of congestion, especially valuable in lower extremity reconstructions. 20 ● Reduced donor site morbidity: muscle-sparing harvest minimizes risk of hernia, weakness, and aesthetic deformity. 5 , 6 Donor Site Morbidity Our pooled donor site morbidity rate (2.78%) is low and aligns with existing literature. 21 Most complications resulted from closure under tension, infection, or hematoma. Postoperative Complications Overall complication rate (14.56%) parallels reports from breast reconstruction (range: 8.3%–29.2%), 7 , 22 – 25 underscoring the DIEP flap’s consistent safety profile across regions. Patient Selection and Alternatives Although the DIEP flap demonstrates high versatility, patient selection remains critical regarding tissue bulk. In our analysis, venous congestion was a leading vascular complication, a risk that can be exacerbated by compression of a bulky flap in a tight anatomical compartment. For defects requiring thin, pliable coverage (such as the distal lower extremity, hand, or scalp), the natural thickness of the DIEP may prove disadvantageous. Conversely, for vaginal and pelvic reconstruction, the bulk of the DIEP flap serves a protective function by obliterating pelvic dead space, though surgeons must be vigilant regarding the risk of stenosis typically caused by scar contracture or insufficient skin paddle width.
full_text_sections.LIMITATIONS: LIMITATIONS This study has several limitations: ● Predominance of case series and reports introduces publication bias, potentially favoring successful outcomes. ● Small sample sizes in some subgroups limit the statistical power. ● Inconsistent reporting across studies restricted detailed pooled analysis of complications. ● Funnel plot symmetry suggests low bias, but inherent limitations remain. Risk of bias was assessed using the Cochrane tool. ( See appendix, Supplemental Digital Content 2 , which displays the assessment of risk of bias in included studies, using the Cochrane risk-of-bias-tool, https://links.lww.com/PRSGO/E973 .) Additional analyses to explore heterogeneity were performed; however, the overall I 2 remained low.
full_text_sections.CONCLUSIONS: CONCLUSIONS The DIEP flap, although historically established for breast reconstruction, demonstrates excellent survival rates and low donor site morbidity across nonbreast regions. This meta-analysis supports its broader use in complex reconstructions requiring large, well-vascularized tissue with minimal donor site morbidity.
full_text_sections.DISCLOSURE: DISCLOSURE The authors have no financial interest to declare in relation to the content of this article.
